data.title: IYRI: Covered Call REIT ETF, Strong Distribution Yield, Short, Adequate Track-Record
data.description:
The NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) is an actively-managed covered call REIT ETF, tracking the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index, and writing covered calls on a portion of its holdings. IYRI's covered call strategy boosts the fund's distribution yield to 11.1% while reducing upside potential. The net effect on a total return basis is dependent on the frequency and magnitude of bull and bear markets, among other factors, and has been close to zero since inception.

In my opinion, IYRI's strong distribution yield and reasonable track-record make the fund a buy. It's not a strong opinion or rating, as the fund's overall investment thesis and benefits to shareholders don't seem all that strong.

The iShares US Real Estate ETF (IYR) tracks the same index as IYRI, without covered calls. As such, I'll be comparing the ETFs throughout the article.

IYRI - Quick Overview

IYRI is an actively-managed covered call REIT ETF. Its REIT investments are roughly equivalent to those of IYR, minus tracking error. IYRI currently invests in around 60 REITs, the largest of these are as follows:

IYRI writes covered calls on a portion of its holdings, increasing the fund's distribution yield and somewhat reducing upside potential. Outperformance is dependent on the frequency and magnitude of bull and bear markets, among other factors. Expect the fund to outperform when markets are flat or drifting downward, due to its distributions. Expect underperformance during bull markets, due to its reduced upside potential. Importantly, bull markets include recoveries, as price gains tend to be quite high during these. Other factors play a role too, including timing and the overall effectiveness of the fund's covered call strategy.

IYRI is functionally equivalent to investing in IYR, and writing covered calls on a portion of its holdings. Doing so has several important implications for investors. Let's have a closer look at some of these.

IYRI - Benefits and Positives

Strong 11.1% Distribution Rate

IYRI sports a 11.1% distribution rate, a strong amount on an absolute basis, significantly higher than that of both REIT indexes and the S&P 500, somewhat lower than other Neos income ETFs.

IYRI's distributions are backed by underlying REIT distributions and covered call premiums both. REIT distributions amount to somewhere between 2.2%, as per IYR's dividend yield above, and 3.2%, as per IYRI's SEC yield. I used bolded text to emphasize that these are different funds.

SEC yields are standardized metrics of income, and do not take into consideration option premiums, unlike traditional dividend or distribution yields.

IYRI's covered calls generate around 7.9-8.9% in premiums, from subtracting SEC yields from distribution yields (hopefully the rule of three here is evident enough).

IYRI's strong distribution yield is a significant benefit for shareholders and could lead to outperformance depending on market conditions. Which brings me to my next point.

Adequate Performance Track-Record

IYRI's performance track-record seems adequate, albeit quite short, with the fund matching the performance of its benchmark since inception, underperforming the S&P 500. Results are as follows:

IYRI's performance relative to the S&P 500 is explained by recent REIT underperformance.

IYRI's performance relative to its REIT benchmark seems broadly in-line with expectations. Specifically, IYRI's covered call strategy should result in outperformance during flat or down markets and underperformance during bull markets. Conditions for REITs since IYRI's inception seem about average, with annual returns of around 10%. I would expect covered calls to not have a significant impact on total returns under average market conditions, as has been the case.

Moving forward, IYRI's absolute returns are strongly dependent on REIT returns and option prices/premiums, the latter of these are dependent on market volatility. IYRI's returns relative to broader REIT indexes are mostly dependent on the frequency and magnitude of any bull and bear markets. Option prices also play a role in the fund's relative performance, as does the timing of these.

I'm not personally expecting or forecasting significant outperformance or underperformance for IYRI moving forward. In the short-term, this is because I don't really see any short-term catalysts for significant price gains or losses for REITs. Long-term, REIT returns should be about average, which shouldn't have a significant impact on the fund's covered call strategy or relative performance. Be that as it may, the fund's strong, double-digit distribution yield remains an important benefit for shareholders.

IYRI - Risks and Downsides

Reduced Upside Potential

IYRI's covered call strategy somewhat reduces upside potential. I was unable to find detailed figures, and I could have sworn Neos provided these. In any case, the prospectus mentions that the fund could write calls on the entirety of its holdings, so it could reduce upside potential to approximately zero. Few ETFs are that aggressive, and IYRI doesn't seem to be the exception, with some periods of positive, albeit reduced, capital gains. As an example, IYRI's share price is up 2.2% YTD, compared to 8.0% for IYR. It is a sizable reduction in gains, but gains were still positive.

Moving forward, I would expect something akin to the above: below-average capital gains for IYRI when REIT prices increase.

An important implication of the above is that covered calls tend to experience below-average price recoveries following a downturn or bear market. Luck and timing also plays a role here, depending on when options are rolled over, as doing so is akin to locking-in losses (obviously much depends on the specifics of the options, but the general point still applies).

Some covered call ETFs see zero, or close to zero, long-term capital gains, as their upside potential is significantly reduced. I'm not sure that this should apply to IYRI, although I don't have sufficient information to know for certain, and obviously much depends on future market conditions. For what it's worth, IYRI's share price is down 1.4% since inception, compared to 10.4% gains for IYR.

On the other hand, other Neos covered call ETFs have seen their prices increase since inception, albeit not by much.

Circling back to IYRI, I believe that price stability is the base case scenario, with the possibility of small long-term gains, but also losses, depending on the frequency and magnitude of bull and bear markets. Although this might not sound all that informative, I wouldn't say the same of broader equity or REIT indexes, which are likelier to see long-term price gains (because equity investors are entitled to their fair share of company profits, and investors wouldn't buy equities with negative expected returns, NPV, etc.).

High 0.68% Expense Ratio

IYRI is a relatively pricey ETF, with a 0.68% expense ratio. Expenses directly and necessarily reduce a fund's income, yield, and total returns. Although IYRI's expense ratio isn't excessive for a covered call ETF, it is definitely higher than average, an important negative for shareholders.

In my opinion, and considering IYRI's strategy and portfolio, the fund's expenses are unlikely to be a decisive factor in its absolute or relative performance in the short or medium-term.

As an example, IYRI has underperformed YTD because REITs have seen sizable price gains, more than enough to outweigh the fund's distributions.

Expenses obviously didn't help but were only a minor factor in the above. IYRI is about 0.30% more expensive than IYR, which meant around 0.06% in reduced gains YTD, barely consequential.

Expenses are a consistent drag on the fund's performance, are of great long-term importance, and increase the probability of (slight) long-term underperformance. For some investors, cheaper index ETFs might be the better choice, but these are rarely going to offer double-digit distribution yields like IYRI.

Narrow Portfolio

IYRI focuses on REITs, a specific sector within the broader equity or real estate space. In general, I prefer more diversified ETFs, to reduce risk. This is of particular importance when discussing covered call ETFs, as these sometimes have difficulties recovering from bear markets, so avoiding excessive, outsized losses is of great importance.

In my opinion, the issues above are not a deal-breaker, as IYRI does provide some diversification within the REIT space, and as investors can always choose to increase their diversification at the portfolio level. Nevertheless, these issues are significant enough that I prefer the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) over IYRI. Seems like the safer choice, and due remember that both ETFs have reduced upside potential.

Conclusion

IYRI is a REIT covered call ETF. In my opinion, IYRI's strong 11.1% distribution yield and good performance track-record outweigh its 0.68% expense ratio and reduced upside potential, making the fund a buy.

data.title: SMH: Outsized Exposure To Winners Triggers Outsized Rally - Buy Upon Dip
data.description:
I previously covered VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) in January 2026, discussing its diversified exposure to the multi-year AI spending trends.

In this article, I shall discuss why SMH remains a Great Buy upon a dip, thanks to its outsized exposure to AI winners.

SMH Enjoys Winning Allocation Strategy

Since my last Buy rating, SMH has outperformed with a +19.5% price return compared to the wider market at +1.4%, with a similar development also observed in its semiconductor ETF peers in varying degrees.

1. SMH's Recent Performance

Between my last and current article, the memory/storage sector has experienced a steep correction in the second half of March 2026, after "investors took profits after a historic run."

Even then, Micron's (MU) V shaped stock price action at the time of writing underscores the resilient demand for memory offerings, as the early Q1'26 earnings season also reiterates the durability of the cloud spending trends.

Beyond the wider semiconductor sector's recent pessimism, SMH continues to deliver outsized returns as well, thanks to its similarly outsized exposure to their top five holdings, including:

  • the market leader of general AI accelerator chip - Nvidia (NVDA) at 18.77%,
  • the world's largest foundry by market share - Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) at 11.28%, and
  • the market leader of custom AI accelerator chip - Broadcom (AVGO) at 8.13%,
  • along with the two market leaders of the x86 chips - Intel (INTC) at 5.32% and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at 4.98% of their holdings.

Particularly, readers may want to note that SMH has been able to outperform with an excellent price return of +320.5% since the launch of ChatGPT by OpenAI on November 30, 2022, compared to the wider market at +77% and its semiconductor-focused ETF peers over the same time period, including:

  • SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) at +123.7% and
  • iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) at +229.3%.

While "past performance may not guarantee future results," SMH's outsized returns may be attributed to the top five holdings comprising 48.4% of the ETF's holdings, against XSD at 11.2% (excluding TSM) and SOXX at 31.2%, with it unsurprising that SMH has been able to outperform their more well balanced ETF peers.

2. SMH's ETF Prospects

As an ETF, SMH at current trading price of $454.80 has been able to perform near to its NAV figure of $454.81 as of April 16, 2026, with this number not too far from their historical "Performance Differential (NAV - Index)" of no more than -0.43 over the past one year.

The same has been observed in SMH trading at a premium to its NAV at 99 days in calendar year 2025, compared to XSD at 63 days and SOXX at 108 days, with the premium reflecting "investor optimism, as they anticipate an increase in the fund's underlying securities."

SMH's expense ratio of 0.35% is relatively inline with its semiconductor-focused ETFs as well, including XSD at 0.35% and SOXX at 0.34%, albeit higher than its tech-heavy peers, ie: QQQ at 0.18%, along with the wider market at 0.09%.

On the other hand, readers must note the cyclical risks from SMH's exposure to the semiconductor sector, since the ETF's return prospects depend on the hyperscalers' sustained spending trends during the multi-year cloud super cycle and therefore, potentially triggering volatilities once this investment cycle tapers.

As a result, I urge readers to diversify their capital across other sectors and/or ETFs for a more balanced portfolio prospect, allowing them to mitigate the potential risks from:

  • the semiconductor sector's prior underperformance between February 2024 and June 2025 along with
  • the rotation into value stocks in January/February 2026.

3. SMH's Holdings Discussed

Nvidia and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing

NVDA's prior supercomputer gift to OpenAI may have contributed to the former's highly sticky CUDA ecosystem moat and their consequent market leadership in the general AI chips at 90% share.

Given the projected doubling in the data center capacity by 2030, it is unsurprising that NVDA has been a clear AI beneficiary as observed in the recently raised 2027 guidance for Blackwell/Rubin sales from the prior number of $500B to $1T.

Thanks to the hyperscalers'/neoclouds' growing appetite for AI chips, TSM may also increasingly utilize their expanded global foundry capacity, with the HPC segment increasingly comprising 58% of the former's revenues in FY2025 (+7 points YoY).

TSM's annual report already highlights its top customer (likely NVDA) commanding a higher share of its foundry capacity and the net revenues at 19% in FY2025 (+7 points YoY), page 114 (bottom left).

With the global semiconductor industry expected to "cross roughly $1T in annual semiconductor sales in 2026" (+26.4% YoY) and TSM already raising their FY2026 revenue growth guidance to over +30% YoY (up from the prior number of nearly +30% YoY), it is apparent that both NVDA and TSM are in the forefront of the AI monetization race.

Broadcom

NVDA's higher pricing power through the higher selling prices may also be why numerous hyperscalers have attempted to diversify their AI chip supply chain with custom AI accelerators, significantly aided by the supposedly improved price to performance ratio.

These reasons may also be why AVGO has offered an excellent AI semiconductor revenue growth guidance from the annualized H1'26 sum of $38.2B (FQ1'26 performance at $8.4B and FQ2'26 guidance at $10.7B) to the 2027 outlook of over $100B (+161.7% YoY).

With Anthropic (ANTHRO) already signing multiple gigawatts of the next-generation TPU capacity, as with META, I am cautiously confident that AVGO may raise their 2027 guidance in the upcoming earnings call, as they monetize the growing demand for their custom AI chips.

Intel and AMD

INTC may appear to be an outlier amongst its currently more successful AI peers, attributed to the lagging AI accelerator offerings and the still unprofitable foundry ambitions.

This is especially since AMD has been gaining x86 share at the expense of INTC's market share erosion to 70.8% in Q4'25 (-4.6 points YoY), as the former also reports ramping up data center sales and the potential growth opportunities worth approximately $50B in annual sales in China.

These developments have lent credibility to AMD's ambitious Analyst Day 2025's revenue growth target at a CAGR of +35%s and adj EPS growth at +40%s over the next few years, with it signaling their robust, multi-year AI growth prospects.

On the other hand, things may change for INTC in the intermediate term, as they join "the Terafab project alongside billionaire Elon Musk's companies SpaceX (SPACE), xAI (X.AI), and Tesla (TSLA) to help refactor silicon fab technology," with the latter's multi-year growth ambitions across EVs/robots/orbital data centers potentially contributing to INTC's reversal prospects from 2029 onwards.

Combined with INTC's integration into NVIDIA NVLink across the hyperscaler, enterprise, and consumer end markets, I can understand why the market has been rather exuberant about the former's renewed growth opportunities.

SMH Remains A Buy Upon Any Dip

For now, SMH has enjoyed a rich +24.9% rally since the March 2026 bottom, thanks to the top five holdings' robust prospects and their consequently outsized recovery after the recent pessimism.

The overly done rally over the past two weeks is also why SMH has entered overbought zones by the time of writing, as observed in the inflated RSIs, with it remaining to be seen if the top five holdings and the ETF may hold on to their recent gains.

This is especially since I am observing a notable divergence in SMH's top three holdings' stock price metrics after the recent break out, such as:

  • NVDA currently trading at $198.87 near to my fair value estimates of $187.90, based on the 1Y P/E mean of ~29x and the annualized FQ4'26 adj EPS performance of $6.48 (+82% YoY),
  • TSM currently at $375.10 against my fair value estimates of $320, based on ~23x and $13.96 (+58.3% YoY),
  • AVGO currently at $396.72 against my fair value estimates of $287, based on ~35x and $8.20 (+28.1% YoY), respectively,

with it apparent that the latter two have been overly buoyed from the recent bottom, while explaining the ETF's overbought technical indicators and the expensive P/E of 43.03x against the wider market at 39.08x.

Given the potential volatility, it may be more prudent to wait for a moderate correction for an improved margin of safety, despite the holdings' structurally robust, profitable growth prospects.

If anything, the peak and trough price action during the two trading cycles in Q4'25 and Q1'26 (as highlighted in red arrows) imply that SMH is likely to return part of its recent gains to retest the prior February 2026 resistance levels of $420s in the intermediate term.

Therefore, while SMH may deliver long-term capital appreciation prospects ahead, as observed in the top three holdings' rich upside potential discussed below, I am of the opinion that it may be more prudent to wait for my Buy Zones to materialize for an improved margin of safety:

  • NVDA may deliver an outsized stock price rally of +97% to my long-term price target of $394.40, based on the consensus FY2029 adj EPS estimates of $13.60 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +41.8%),
  • TSM by +52% to $562.80, based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $24.47 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +31.9%), and
  • AVGO by +98% to $789.60, based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $22.56 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +49%).

data.title: A $3 Trillion Question: How Fragile Is Private Credit?
data.description:
By James Picerno

Private credit has gone from a niche backwater to an increasingly mainstream allocation, but investors are discovering that its headline attraction - lofty yields - may come with real, often underappreciated, risk.

Minds have become more focused on the risk aspect lately as questions about the viability of the market come under critical scrutiny. In years past, worries about private credit could be ignored by most investors, but the industry for lending by private equity firms has boomed, especially in the post-pandemic years. Critics are now asking whether this former backwater is becoming too big to fail and poses a threat to the wider economy.

The value of debt financing beyond conventional borrowing channels (banks and the bond market) in the US was $1.34 trillion in 2024, roughly five times higher than in 2009, according to the Federal Reserve. Some estimates now peg the figure above $3 trillion-plus in 2026.

A Boom That’s Finally Drawing Scrutiny

Few observers saw the growth of alternative lending sources as a problem until recently, when investor redemptions began rising sharply, persuading some managers to limit withdrawals. One trigger for turning cautious on private credit is the industry’s hefty lending to software companies, which have come under pressure lately on the view that Artificial Intelligence threatens their business models.

Right or wrong, a more cautious view on the outlook for software firms is reverberating back into private credit. The fear is that a stress-driven correction in private credit, given its breadth and depth in the financial ecosystem, could create a liquidity shock on Wall Street and beyond.

Some analysts point to rising stress signals, such as an increasing default rate among US private credit borrowers. But in a sign that the worst may have passed, Fitch Ratings estimates that the default rate in US private credit declined to 5.4% in February, down from January’s 5.8%, the highest since August 2024.

Even if you never touch private credit, the industry is worth tracking because stress in this opaque corner of the market can transmit outward - through bank funding lines, credit spreads, and real economy financing - and turn a niche disruption into broader financial and economic strain. A useful starting point for monitoring sentiment is watching two ETFs that have been plying these waters.

The funds in the chart below represent two measures of the private credit industry. The Virtus Private Credit Strategy ETF (VPC) tracks the Indxx Private Credit Index, which is composed of US-listed business development companies (BDCs) and closed-end funds (CEFS) focused on private credit. The VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) targets the MVIS US Business Development Companies Index, a proxy for the performance of publicly traded BDCs, which are funds that invest in small- and mid-sized companies.

Note the recent upturn in both funds, which may be a sign that the market is beginning to look through the recent turbulence. Some high-profile observers of the market appear to be softening concerns about the risk outlook. JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon said this week that he’s “not particularly worried” about his firm’s $50 billion allocation to private credit.

The Signals That Could Matter Most For Private Credit

A rise in interest rates could change minds, but here too, the latest numbers suggest the market has steadied. The 10-year Treasury yield, for example, has increased moderately since the start of the war with Iran, but the current 4.32% yield remains in a middling range for the trailing one-year period. A rise above the recent peak (roughly 4.45%) could be a new warning sign.

Perhaps the critical variable is when - or if - banks dramatically pull back their credit lines, a source of liquidity that has been instrumental to date in financing new deals and managing cash flow. Those lines of credit may come under closer scrutiny if more investors try to liquidate their private credit holdings.

What might trigger a new round of investor withdrawals? There are many possibilities, but a sharp rise in Treasury yields is near the top of the list. That scenario may become more plausible if incoming inflation data is hotter than expected due to the supply-side energy shock unleashed by the war.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Realty Income: An Attractive Holding In A Dividend Portfolio, But Fully Valued
data.description:
When I last wrote about Realty Income (O), I rated it a buy due to its robust tenants portfolio, attractive valuation, and its monthly dividend payments. Since then, however, the macroeconomic environment has largely changed - with the outbreak of the war in the Middle East. Energy prices skyrocketed and consumer confidence fell to historic lows. At the same time, O's share price increased significantly. I believe at the current valuation, and considering the macro landscape, more caution is warranted - therefore I downgrade my rating from buy to hold. My aim today is to give you a balanced view with my reasoning why I find the downgrade necessary

1. Fundamentals and the macro landscape

Realty Income continues to have a strong and well-diversified tenant portfolio, with grocery- and convenience stores making up more than 20% of their annual base rent. The tenant base is also diversified geographically. I find this important because if one sector struggles, the revenue stream from the other may remain unaffected, helping O to still achieve its financial goals.

As a result of the solid tenant base, O is also consistently able to achieve high and stable occupancy rates - staying at around 98% in the past decade.

I do have a bit of a concern now, however. Consumer confidence in the United States has hit record low levels - mainly driven by the outbreak of the war in Iran, resulting in elevated energy prices and an uncertain interest rate environment. Low consumer confidence generally slows the spending on discretionary, non-essential goods and services. While O is not likely to be affected directly, their tenants may see significant headwinds. The industries that are most likely to be affected are home improvement, restaurants, and automotive services.

It is also important to note that O's leases are triple net leases, meaning that the tenant is responsible for the property tax payments, insurance, and maintenance. This is attractive from a perspective that even if inflationary pressures kick in due to elevated energy prices, tenants will need to bear the increased costs of the property maintenance. Naturally, this puts more pressure on the tenants from a cost side.

Last, but not least, I would like to point out the risks inherent to O's capital structure. As a REIT, O has a significant amount of debt on its balance sheet - roughly $30 billion, compared to the $440 million cash on hand.

The firm's balance sheet is relatively strong and has a stable credit rating from both S&P Global and Moody's. There is, however, a large portion of debt maturing in the coming 3 years. These debts will likely need to be refinanced, and the cost of debt is going to be largely dependent on the overall interest rate environment.

While in late 2024 and 2025 we saw several rate cuts, the expectation for 2026 changed significantly. Before the outbreak of the war in the Middle East, several rate cuts were expected this year. Now, the outlook is uncertain, and at one point even potential rate hikes were in the cards. For this reason, I believe that caution is warranted, even if the well-diversified tenant base still looks attractive and stable.

2. Dividends and valuation

O has always been on the radar of dividend investors, due to the monthly payouts and the attractive dividend growth over the years. The firm currently pays a monthly dividend of $0.2705 per share, equivalent to an annual yield of roughly 5%.

Since 1994, dividend growth was 4.2% CAGR, but in recent years growth has slowed. Dividend growth TTM is 2.2%.

In my previous writing, I used a dividend discount model with a required rate of return of 7.49%, and a perpetual dividend growth rate of 1.9%, yielding a fair value of $63 per share.

Let us now update this model with more recent estimates. Currently, the firm's cost of equity is estimated to be 7.72% - so I will be using this as a required rate of return. For the dividend growth rate in perpetuity, I will be using the previously mentioned 2.2% growth.

These input parameters yield a fair value of $65 per share, which is roughly in line with the current market price.

Considering the deterioration of the macroeconomic landscape, the elevated energy prices, and the uncertain interest rate dynamics in the coming months and quarters, I believe that the current market price does not allow for enough margin of safety to justify an investment now from a valuation point of view. I still like O from a fundamental point of view, and I still believe that it is an attractive holding in a dividend portfolio. At the current levels, however, I do not find starting a new position or adding to an existing one attractive.

Conclusions

Realty Income's tenant base remains well-diversified and stable, with 98+% occupancy rates. The low level of consumer confidence is not likely to impact O directly but is likely going to create headwinds for tenants in the home improvement, restaurant, and auto service industries.

Realty Income, just like most REITs, has a debt-heavy balance sheet, making the firm's financial performance highly dependent on the interest rate environment. Due to elevated energy prices, and the corresponding inflationary pressures, interest rate dynamics became uncertain, potentially leading to high rates for longer, creating direct headwinds for O.

Based on a dividend discount model, O's stock appears to be fully valued - as the estimated fair value is $65 per share, roughly equivalent to the current share price.

I do believe it is still an attractive holding in a dividend portfolio, but in my view, it is not the best time to add to an existing position or to start a new position now.

For these reasons, I downgrade O from buy to hold.

data.title: Driehaus Small Cap Growth Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The March quarter started off well as January and February were strong opening months for the U.S. equity market. Key drivers of the market were favorable, including: strong economic growth, robust earnings (especially for small caps), the Federal Reserve's rate cutting cycle, tax cuts (as the so called Big Beautiful Bill became effective in 2026), expanded deregulation, the ISM PMI Manufacturing index entering expansion territory (above 50), and inflation gradually easing along with long-term interest rates. These positives outweighed negatives such as the stalled labor market and growing concerns about private credit. As a result, through February, equities were up for the year, market breadth expanded and small caps outperformed large caps. Then on February 28th, the US & Israel launched strikes on Iran, initiating the Iran War. This was in response to growing potential threats by the Iranian regime and the recent brutal attacks on its own people.

The Iran War

The Iran War has dramatically changed the equity market environment for the time being. Equities fell sharply in the month of March as the price of crude oil nearly doubled, interest rates rose, the US dollar rallied, economic uncertainty spiked and investor sentiment plunged. WTI crude oil increased from $65 two days before the war to over $100 by the end of March just over a month later. Other key global crude oil benchmarks, such as, Brent, Oman, and Dubai were even higher. On most individual trading days in March, the price of crude oil was the critical factor. Favorable war headlines caused the price of crude oil to fall and equities to rally in an inverse relationship. However, the opposite was true on most days, where negative war headlines and higher crude prices caused equity multiples to compress. Overall, the fog of war remains thick.

As of one week into April, the US and Iran have agreed to a two-week ceasefire. The ceasefire is a clear positive that for the time being removing the threat of a highly escalatory bombing campaign on Iran's power and economic infrastructure and avoids ground troops and other dire outcomes. It also creates a window for the resumption of ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz (SoH) albeit under Iran's new exorbitant toll system, potentially providing relief from the global energy shock. However, the ceasefire is fragile. Trust between US and Iran is low. The peace plans put forth by both sides have several demands that seem unrealistic. However, they are a starting point for continued negotiations. The SoH is the most important factor for crude oil, the global economy and the equity market and it remains mostly closed. The US says the ceasefire's durability hinges on the Strait reopening fully. A failure in talks could see a rapid resumption of hostilities, but successful talks could de-escalate the broader crisis and would be a major positive for the equity market.

Trump's decision to accept the ceasefire and push out his most recent deadline again is a reflection of the limits of his own appetite for economic and political damage through further escalation rather than substantive progress on a nuclear deal, regional proxies, sanctions and real regime change, key issues which remain extremely difficult to resolve. Nonetheless, for the markets and the global economy, the ceasefire and negotiations are a major positive. It is more likely than not that the fragile two-week ceasefire will be extended longer term, though there are many conflicting headlines that will likely continue to create severe daily market volatility. For instance, Iran has continued to bomb multiple middle east countries after the ceasefire started and Iran is objecting to Israel's escalating attacks on Lebanon, but it does appear the US and Iran have stopped attacking each other.

Before this ceasefire, Trump dramatically increased his threats on the Iranian Regime and the IRGC (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corp) demanding they open the Strait. His ultimatum to open the Strait or face attacks on Iran's power plants, bridges and other key infrastructure grew louder and louder. These threats likely have contributed to the tentative ceasefire agreement. It had been our base case that one way or another Trump would declare victory relatively soon and that would cause the equity market to recover as the US economy should continue to grow albeit with higher inflation due to energy prices in the near term.

The Strait of Hormuz remains a critical bottleneck, both in terms of US-Iran negotiations and for the global economy. If it can fully re-open, its impact on global crude oil prices and supply will lessen. Outside of the Strait, energy infrastructure has been severely damaged by Iran in key Persian Gulf countries disrupting the supply of crude oil, natural gas, aluminum and key petrochemicals. Even with a re-opened Strait, the supply of these commodities will be impacted for months and potentially years as it will take time to repair production facilities, refineries, and other key infrastructure.

Other Key Macro Considerations

Artificial Intelligence (AI) continues to be the major theme driving the performance of multiple parts of the market. The market has separated many companies within various industries into AI beneficiaries or those disrupted by AI. For instance, many tech hardware companies and industrial companies are benefitting from the AI capex cycle which is fueling data center growth as well as the utility capex cycle driving an upgrade and expansion of the US electrical grid. On the other hand, software, IT services, business services and many other digital or white-collar businesses are being viewed by the market as disrupted by AI. Overall, AI compute demand continues to exceed supply which gives confidence to the sustainability of the hyperscaler capex data center buildout.

Outside of AI, many cyclical groups are performing well within industrials, materials, energy, transports, and semiconductors. This is a function of various favorable economic trends. The ISM PMI manufacturing index posted its third consecutive month above 50 (expansionary), which comes after a prolonged period of contraction. We view this expansion as likely sustainable given signs of strong demand in multiple industries and themes, as well as a general low level of inventory across the economy. Together this should drive further production to meet demand and replenish inventory stocks. There are also positive signs of improving demand via accelerating new order growth in the PMI reports (the strongest since early 2023), as well as recent strength in trucking, bank loan, and construction data.

Key risks beyond crude oil and the war do remain. One is the growing stress in the private credit markets. Equity investors have had growing concerns about cracks in these opaque private finance markets. Blue Owl Capital, Inc. has been at the epicenter of these concerns as it and others have seen elevated requests for withdrawals from their funds, especially from retail investors. Credit crises often start out as small cracks and then grow to be larger than was initially expected. So, the market is closely watching for signs of contagion, and potential signs of systematic problems impacting banks, insurance companies, business development companies, and investment banks. A key question will be, has the weakness in the software industry or the huge expansion in the data center market had a negative impact on private credit. This upcoming earnings season will be critical to monitor to see if there are signs of further stress in private credit within the banking sector.

Another concern is the labor market. For an otherwise growing economy, job creation is unusually weak, though it was stronger than expected in the month of March. Healthcare and construction are the only major parts of the economy creating new jobs. There is a shortage of skilled, blue-collar labor but there are signs of excess supply in knowledge based or white-collar jobs particularly in technology, finance and among recent college graduates. The fear is that AI is beginning to negatively impact job creation. For those unemployed it is taking longer to find a job. Overall, initial jobless claims are still low historically, but so is new job creation. The result is a still tight but very stagnant labor market. The unemployment rate of 4.3% is low but there are risks the labor market will weaken further, which is driving concerns about the so-called K-shaped economy.

Valuations remain appealing for small caps, especially compared to large caps. The investment case for small caps stocks is compelling when looking at small cap earnings, which have been robust and accelerating since the June quarter of last year. Also, small cap earnings are expected to outgrow large cap earnings for the next few quarters as shown in the bar chart below. History further shows that small caps typically outperform during the first five years or more off a new market cycle, which we believe began a year ago on April 9th, 2025, when the market bottomed post Liberation Day. Additionally, small caps and equities overall consistently do well for at least the 12 months following the beginning of a rate cutting cycle by the Fed.

Exhibit 1: S&P 600 vs. S&P 500 Equal-Weight Y/Y Pct. Change In EPS

Source: Strategas, Bloomberg

Performance Review

The Driehaus Small Cap Growth (DVSMX) strategy outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Growth Index, by approximately 250 basis points for the first quarter of 2026. The strategy declined by 0.31% versus a decline of 2.81% for the Russell 2000 Growth, a gain of 0.82% for the Russell 2000, and a decline of 4.60% for the S&P 500.1 From a sector perspective, the top three contributing sectors on a relative basis were technology, industrials, and energy while the bottom three sectors on a relative basis were financials, consumer discretionary, and materials.

1 The performance data represents the strategy's composite of small cap growth accounts managed by Driehaus Capital Management LLC (DCM). These returns are estimated for the period as the underlying accounts' data is yet to be reconciled to the custodian bank. Net of fee returns reflect the payment of advisory fees and in some instances, other fees and expenses such as administrative and custodian fees while the gross of fee returns do not. Both are net of brokerage commissions charged to the accounts and reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings. The performance data shown above represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

Sector Attribution

From a sector perspective, the top two contributing sectors on a relative basis for the quarter were information technology and industrials. The financials and consumer discretionary sectors performed negatively on a relative basis for the quarter.

Exhibit 2: Sector Attribution Relative to Benchmark

Source: Driehaus. Note: The sector attribution relative to benchmark for each MSCI/GICS Sector is equal to the sum of the individual Attribution Effects for that MSCI/GICS Sector. The cash weighting at 3/31/26 was 2.3%.

Sector Commentary – Contributors:

Information Technology

Information Technology was the largest relative sector outperformer and second largest gainer in absolute terms. Our technology holdings gained 6.7% versus a decline of 9.8% for the benchmark. Strong stock selection was the primary driver of outperformance along with being overweight strong sub-industries such as semiconductors and communications equipment as well as having no exposure to out of favor areas such as enterprise software and IT services.

Tech hardware holdings related to semiconductors, high bandwidth memory, optical equipment, and other data center infrastructure areas performed well as they are seeing strong earnings due to improving demand and rising AI-related capital spending and data center growth. Specifically, two optical component suppliers were the top contributors as they reported strong earnings driven by growing AI data center buildouts. Overall, the quarter's performance and positioning favor hardware companies benefiting from AI and the avoidance of software and IT service companies that are under pressure as they are potentially being disrupted by competitive AI threats. The strategy slightly decreased exposure to the tech sector during the quarter and maintains an underweight exposure overall.

Industrials

Industrials was the second largest contributor on a relative basis and the largest on an absolute basis. Our industrial holdings gained 7.5% versus 5.6% for the benchmark. The strategy increased exposure to the sector during the quarter and continued to maintain an overweight position. Outperformance was driven by strong earnings and improving outlooks across a variety of industries including commercial aerospace, defense, satellite services, trucking, engineering & construction and other AI infrastructure and electrical grid related holdings. A satellite services holding was the top performer as it reported strong backlog growth, accelerating revenue growth and better than expected EBITDA. The second-best performer was a commercial aerospace company that saw strength due to a new product driving its addressable market expansion as well raised guidance on its earnings report.

Energy

Energy performed well as it generated the third largest outperformance on both a relative and absolute basis. Outperformance was due to being overweight the sector as our energy holdings gained 17.3% versus 26.1% for the benchmark. Energy was the market's strongest sector during the quarter as the Iran War dramatically impacted crude oil and other commodity prices as supply concerns grew due to the damage inflicted on Persian Gulf energy infrastructure and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, blocking 20% of the world's crude oil from reaching global markets. The portfolio saw strong gains in holdings in oilfield services, uranium, and exploration & production. An offshore oil services company and a uranium mining company were the top contributors as both reported strong earnings, favorable industry supply-demand conditions, and a favorable outlook driving performance.

Sector Commentary – Detractors:

Financials

Financials was the largest sector detractor on a relative basis and second largest on an absolute basis. Our financial holdings declined 22.5% versus a decline of 5.3% for the benchmark. While the strategy's large sector underweight provided some favorable offset, stock underperformance remained the primary headwind as weak performance occurred across the sector, including in specialty finance, payments, capital markets, and insurance holdings. Sector weakness was broad based due to macro concerns such as the Iran War, private credit concerns and rising interest rates. We reduced our sector exposure further during the quarter.

Consumer Discretionary

Consumer Discretionary was the second largest detractor on a relative basis and the largest in absolute terms. Our consumer discretionary holdings declined 13.5% versus a decline of 5.9% for the benchmark. Performance was impacted by stock performance results across specialty retail, apparel, leisure products, and restaurant holdings as macro concerns weighed on sentiment. We reduced sector exposure during the quarter, finishing March modestly underweight versus the benchmark.

Materials

Materials was the third largest sector detractor though it saw only minor declines on an absolute basis. Our materials holdings declined by 6.5% outperforming the large 23.8% decline for the benchmark. The portfolio slightly increased its exposure to the sector during the quarter but maintained an underweight during the quarter. In the month of March, the sector's weakness was broad as precious metals, copper and rare earth elements and other critical materials experienced declines due to the Iran War.

Strategy Overview and Positioning

In terms of portfolio positioning, by sector, industrials remained our largest absolute weight, followed by health care, information technology, and energy. On a relative basis, the strategy is overweight industrials, health care, and energy, while the strategy is underweight financials, information technology, and real estate. The biggest sector changes in terms of relative weight were increases in industrials and energy and decreases in health care and financials.

Exhibit 3: Variation in Ending Weight

Source: Driehaus. Note: Change in relative weight is the difference between the change in ending weight from the previous quarter. The cash weighting at 3/31/26 was 2.3%.

Market Outlook

Overall, the macro data suggests the economy is resilient and continues to grow nicely. As mentioned above there are signs of strength across multiple industries and themes but there are risks surrounding the war, crude oil prices, private credit, and the labor market. The outlook for further Fed rate cuts is complicated by these risks, but investors are still expecting additional cuts this year. There will be a new Fed Chair soon when the new nominee Kevin Warsh is confirmed. The market will have to adjust to Warsh and historically the market consistently tests a new Fed Chair resulting in a market correction or some market stress early in a new Chair's term. However, Warsh is experienced, very qualified and he should be another in a long line of strong, capable leaders of the FOMC.

We believe if the fog of war begins to clear and the new tenuous ceasefire agreement proves sustainable the impact on the markets will be very positive, though the damaged energy infrastructure in the Middle East will impact the supply of energy products globally and the Strait of Hormuz needs to be re-opened soon. In sum, if peace prevails, the market will likely recover strongly.

In terms of portfolio positioning, we have an attractive mix of secular and cyclical growth holdings. By sector, industrials is our largest absolute weight, followed by healthcare, technology, energy, and consumer discretionary. On a relative basis, the strategy is overweight industrials, health care, and energy. The strategy is underweight financials, technology, and real estate.

Terms

Active share represents the share of portfolio holdings that differ from the benchmark index holdings. Beta is a measure of a portfolio's volatility. A beta of 1.00 implies perfect historical correlation of movement with the market. A higher beta manager will rise and fall more rapidly than the market, whereas a lower beta manager will rise and fall slower. Information Ratio (IR) measures a portfolio manager's ability to generate excess returns relative to a benchmark, but also attempts to identify the consistency of the investor. This ratio will identify if a manager has beaten the benchmark by a lot in a few months or a little every month. The higher the IR the more consistent a manager is and consistency is an ideal trait. R-Squared is a statistical measure that represents the percentage of a fund or security's movements that can be explained by movements in a benchmark index. For fixed-income securities, the benchmark is the T-bill. For equities, the benchmark is the S&P 500. Standard Deviation is a measure of the average deviations of a return series from its mean; often used as a measure of portfolio volatility. A large standard deviation implies that there have been large swings or volatility in the manager's return series. Tracking Error is a divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark. This is often in the context of a hedge or mutual fund that did not work as effectively as intended, creating an unexpected profit or loss instead.
